info.name: Filmpire API
info.description:
Complete API collection for testing all Filmpire microservices through the API Gateway, with request tracing.

Infrastructure Flow:
1. Request → API Gateway (localhost:8080)
2. Gateway → Eureka Service Discovery (localhost:8761)
3. Eureka → target service (movie-service:8081, user-service:8082, actor-service:8083)
4. Service → TMDB API (external) / PostgreSQL / MongoDB / Redis as needed
5. Response with caching where applicable

HATEOAS: single-resource GET endpoints (movie/actor details, user profile) advertise navigation via `_links`. Movie details return the HATEOAS resource bare (top-level `_links`); actor details and the user profile stay inside the shared ApiResponse envelope, so their links live under `data._links` instead.

Auth: run '8. User Service - Auth → Register' first (or 'Login' if you already have an account) to populate the access_token/refresh_token collection variables used by every '9. User Service - Profile & Lists' request.

item.name: 8. User Service - Auth

request.description: Create an account. Generates a random username/email via a pre-request script so this can be re-run without colliding with a previous run, then captures access_token/refresh_token for the rest of the collection.

exec: // Generate a fresh, collision-free username/email each run so Register
// can be replayed without a manual 409/400 cleanup step.

request.description: Log in with the credentials created by Register (test_username/test_password collection variables). Run Register at least once first.
Rotate the refresh token: mints a new access/refresh pair and invalidates the presented one.
Revoke the current refresh token. The access token stays valid until its natural expiry.

item.description: Registration, login, token refresh and logout (issue #17)

item.name: 12. Schema-Drift Self-Healing (#46)
item.description:
Verifies ADR-011: a MongoDB document written by an OLDER version of the Movie model must be treated as a cache miss (evicted + re-fetched from TMDB), not surfaced as a 500.

Before ADR-011 this was a permanent failure: the read threw, so save-through never ran, so the bad document was never rewritten — every subsequent request failed identically until someone deleted it by hand.

SETUP (run once, outside Postman — Postman cannot write a shape the app refuses to write):

  podman exec filmpire-mongo mongosh -u admin -p admin123 \
    --authenticationDatabase admin filmpire --quiet --eval '
      db.movies.deleteOne({tmdbId: 550});
      db.movies.insertOne({tmdbId: 550, title: "Fight Club",
        overview: "STALE - pre-ADR-010 document",
        spokenLanguages: ["English", "German"]});'

`spokenLanguages` as an array of strings is the pre-ADR-010 shape; the current model expects embedded SpokenLanguage objects.

Then run this folder. The first request heals the document; the second proves the heal persisted and is served locally.

item.name: Drifted document heals on read (expect 200, not 500)

request.description: Reads a movie whose stored document is in the outdated shape. The service must log the drift, evict the document by query, re-fetch from TMDB and save it in the current shape — returning 200 with real data.

exec: // The whole point of #46: this used to be a permanent 500.
pm.test('Returns 200 rather than 500 on a schema-drifted document', function () {
// If the stale document had been served, overview would still be the
// planted 'STALE - ...' text. Real TMDB data proves the re-fetch ran.
pm.test('Serves freshly re-fetched data, not the stale document', function () {
pm.test('Document is readable in the current model shape', function () {

item.name: Heal is durable (second read served locally)

request.description: The repair must be persisted, not recomputed per request. A fix that re-fetched every time would still return 200 but would silently burn the TMDB rate limit.

exec: pm.test('Consistent data on repeat read', function () {
// Served from MongoDB, so it should be fast.
